steps.title: Map the sediment evidence
steps.content: The passage gives three sediment clues. Most of the material is fine-grained mudstone, which forms by slow settling in low-flow water and points to a lake. Those fine grains show cracking from episodic desiccation, so the lake sometimes dried out. Coarse sandstone intervals point to periods when flowing water, such as streams, was present.

steps.title: Reject lake contradictions
steps.content:
• Choice A - although the area of the Murray Formation experienced a prolonged period of dryness that prevented a lake from forming, water flowing from a distant source was present.: This denies lake formation, but the dominant mudstone points to a prolonged low-flow water environment.
  • Choice C - one or more streams existed at the Murray Formation for an extended period until being replaced by a lake that persisted for only a brief period before permanently drying.: A brief lake cannot explain why most sediment is mudstone, and the passage does not say streams were replaced by a lake.

steps.title: Require all three signals
steps.content:
• Choice D - a stream-fed lake was present at the Murray Formation for an extended period, and although the streams experienced occasional drying, the lake did not.: The cracks are in the fine grains associated with the lake sediment, so the lake did experience drying.
The conclusion must include a prolonged lake, occasional drying, and some stream activity. The answer is B.
